verses.text: When Jesus finished instructing his twelve disciples, he went on to teach and preach in their towns.
But John, hearing in prison about the works of Christ, sent word by his disciples
to ask him, “Are you the one who is to come, or should we look for another?”
Jesus answered, “Go tell John what you hear and see:
the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the poor have good news proclaimed to them.
Blessed is the one who is not offended because of me.”
Now as these were going away, Jesus began to speak to the crowds concerning John: “What did you go out to the wilderness to see? A reed shaken by the wind?
But what did you go out to see? A man dressed in soft clothing? Look, those who wear soft clothing are in the houses of kings.
But why did you go out? To see a prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet.
For this is the one of whom it is written: ‘Behold, I am sending my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way before you.’
Truly I tell you, among those born of women, none has arisen greater than John the Baptist, yet the least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he.
From the days of John the Baptist until now, the kingdom of heaven is advancing under oppression, but the zealous ones are seizing it.
For all the prophets and the law prophesied until John.
“And if you are willing to accept it, he is Elijah who was to come.
Let the one who has ears, listen.”
“To what shall I compare this generation? It is like children sitting in the marketplace, calling to one another:
‘We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not mourn.’
For John came neither eating nor drinking, and they say, ‘He has a demon.’
The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and they say, ‘Look, a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners.’ Yet wisdom is justified by her deeds.”
Then he reproached the cities where most of his miracles had been done, because they did not repent.
“Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the miracles done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes.
But I tell you, it will be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon on the day of judgment than for you.
And you, Capernaum, will you be exalted to heaven? No, you will be brought down to Hades. For if the miracles done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day.
But I tell you, it will be more tolerable for the land of Sodom on the day of judgment than for you.”
At that time, Jesus declared, “I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and intelligent and revealed them to little ones.
Yes, Father, for this was your gracious will.
All things have been handed over to me by my Father, and no one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son wills to reveal him.”

sections.headline: Come to Me, I Will Give You Rest

verses.text: “Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.
Share my labor upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls.
For my way is freeing, and my burden is light.”

footnotes.content: The question “Are you the one who is to come?” reveals John's uncertainty, likely provoked by his imprisonment and unmet expectations. The Greek phrase uses ho erchomenos (“the Coming One”), a messianic title derived from passages like Psalm 118:26 and Daniel 7:13. This title signals hope for divine deliverance—but John's use here implies struggle, not certainty. His honesty reflects the spiritual tension between expectation and reality.

The word translated as “turn away” comes from the Greek verb skandalizō, often rendered “stumble” or “fall away.” However, in modern English, “stumble” has become too soft, and “fall away” risks misinterpretation—as if Jesus were the cause of someone's sin or apostasy. In reality, skandalizō points to the offense or spiritual resistance people feel when confronted with Christ's humility, mercy, or unexpected ways. The rendering “turn away because of me” was chosen to preserve this tension: it describes the heart that recoils from Jesus when He does not meet worldly expectations. This becomes especially clear in context—John the Baptist, imprisoned and doubting, sends messengers to ask if Jesus is truly the One. Rather than rebuke, Jesus responds with blessing: the one who remains faithful, even when God's work unfolds in paradox, is blessed. This translation honors the original meaning while making it spiritually and emotionally accessible to the reader.

The image of a “reed shaken by the wind” evokes instability, cowardice, or conformism—qualities opposite to John the Baptist's prophetic character. In first-century Palestine, reeds were common along the Jordan and easily bent by the breeze. Jesus uses the metaphor rhetorically to ask whether the people expected someone fickle or afraid. The implied answer is no: John was unwavering, even unto imprisonment. The phrase exposes the contrast between public fascination and actual recognition of prophetic truth. By raising this question, Jesus affirms John's moral fortitude and prophetic authority.

Jesus declares John the Baptist to be the greatest of those “born of women”—a Hebrew idiom meaning all human beings. John's greatness lies in his role as the final prophet of the old covenant, the one who directly announced and identified the Christ. Yet paradoxically, the “least in the kingdom of heaven” is said to be greater—not in holiness or courage, but in spiritual position. John stood on the threshold of the new covenant but did not live to witness its fulfillment. Those who enter the kingdom after Christ's resurrection partake in a deeper spiritual reality—sealed by the Spirit, covered by the cross, and joined to the risen life of the Christ. It is a shift in redemptive history: greatness is no longer measured by proximity to prophecy, but by participation in the kingdom Christ has inaugurated.

The Greek verbs biazetai (“is forcefully advancing”) and harpazousin (“are laying hold of it”) paint a picture of intensity and movement. The phrase “the zealous are laying hold of it” was chosen to express not just effort, but the emotional desperation of those who know they cannot afford to miss the kingdom. Jesus is not describing violent aggressors but spiritually awakened people—those whose longing for salvation pushes them past fear, pride, and delay. “Zealous” captures the soul-deep resolve of the poor in spirit, the broken, the hungry, who rush toward the kingdom as their only hope. This interpretation aligns with Luke 16:16, where Jesus says, “everyone is forcing their way into it”—not by violence, but by the urgency of awakened faith. Grammatically, biazetai may be understood either as a middle-voice verb (“the kingdom is forcefully advancing”) or as a passive (“is being attacked”), but in context, the middle fits best. The language points to the disruptive inbreaking of God's reign, met with passionate response by those who recognize its worth.

When Jesus says, “he is Elijah,” he affirms that John the Baptist fulfills the long-awaited role prophesied in Malachi 4:5. The phrase “if you are willing to accept it” reveals that this identity is spiritually perceived rather than universally obvious. The crowd expected Elijah to return in fire, yet he came in camel's hair, calling for repentance. Jesus leaves room for mystery—John may not look like the Elijah of old, but he bears the mantle, the mission, and the spirit of the forerunner. This subtle ambiguity invites the listener to decide whether they are willing to see prophetic fulfillment not by appearance, but by function and divine appointment.

The saying, “We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not mourn,” draws from a children's game mimicking weddings and funerals—occasions for celebration and sorrow. The metaphor rebukes the spiritual stubbornness of Jesus' generation: they refused to respond to either John's somber call to repentance or Jesus' joyful invitation to the kingdom. Like petulant children demanding a different tune, they judged the message based on the mood of the messenger. This reveals a deeper issue: it wasn't the form that offended them, but the truth itself.

The accusation, “Look, a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners,” reflects how critics distorted Jesus' table fellowship to discredit him. “Glutton” and “drunkard” were serious moral charges in Jewish culture (cf. Deuteronomy 21:20), and the phrase “friend of sinners” was used derisively to question his holiness. Yet Jesus embraces the scandal of grace—he did not avoid the unclean, but brought the kingdom to them. The final line, “wisdom is justified by her children,” means that the fruit of his ministry—transformed lives—proves the divine origin of his actions. Truth is not measured by public approval, but by its lasting result.

Jesus rebukes Capernaum with language that echoes Isaiah 14:13–15, where the proud are cast down from heaven to Sheol. The word Hades translates the Greek Hadēs, referring to the realm of the dead—often associated with the Hebrew Sheol. It is not the final place of judgment (Gehenna), but a shadowed domain of spiritual exile and separation from the living. The contrast is sharp: though Capernaum had witnessed great miracles, it will not be exalted to divine glory, but will descend into death's domain. The warning signals both spiritual consequence and eschatological reversal—the proud will be brought low, and the unrepentant judged more severely than even Sodom.

“Wise and intelligent” refers not to true godly wisdom but to those who consider themselves intellectually or spiritually competent—often the educated, self-assured, or religiously elite who depend on their own insight rather than divine revelation. This aligns with Isaiah 29:14 and 1 Corinthians 1:19, where God nullifies the wisdom of the wise and frustrates the discernment of the discerning, emphasizing that self-reliant wisdom stands opposed to God’s revelatory purposes. Paul expands this in 1 Corinthians 1:26–29, showing that God deliberately chooses what the world deems foolish to shame the wise, exalting humble dependence over intellectual pride. The word translated “praise” exomologoumai soi means to confess, thank, or openly acknowledge, not merely to express admiration. Here, it reflects Jesus’ profound agreement with the Father’s will in concealing truth from the self-sufficient and revealing it to the “infants”—those of childlike humility and openness to God’s revelation (cf. Matthew 18:3; Luke 10:21).

Jesus affirms that the Father's choice to reveal divine truths to the humble, rather than to the self-assured, was not random, but rooted in mercy. The Greek word eudokia can mean “good pleasure” or “delight,” but in this context, it points beyond mere satisfaction to the deeper benevolence of God's plan. “Gracious will” captures this nuance: what pleases God is not only wise, but kind — a will shaped by grace. Jesus celebrates not just the hiddenness of the kingdom, but the generosity behind its unveiling. This phrasing makes explicit the Father's joy in lifting the lowly and revealing truth to those open enough to receive it.

The traditional phrasing “Take my yoke upon you” (v. 29) draws on a rich but now largely unfamiliar metaphor. In the first-century Jewish context, a “yoke” (zygos) referred not only to the wooden harness that bound two oxen together for plowing — a symbol of labor, constraint, and submission — but also to a rabbi’s interpretive path, the ethical framework through which one lived out divine truth. This image carried profound spiritual weight, encompassing guidance, discipleship, relationship, empowerment, submission, partnership, liberation, comfort, fittingness, transformation, simplicity, grace, and peace. However, the physical yoke itself, though carefully shaped to fit the necks of the animals, still represented a binding and often burdensome reality. Even a well-carved yoke, intended to distribute the load evenly, still demanded effort and endurance, and an ill-fitting yoke could cause painful sores and strain, becoming a source of suffering. In the same way, a harsh or overly rigid religious system can crush the spirit and weary the soul, placing burdens too heavy to bear. By contrast, Jesus’ path is one of grace and freedom, not mere external obligation. The TTT rendering “Share my labor upon you” (v. 29) and “For my way is freeing and my burden is light” (v. 30) seek to capture this liberating spirit without the cultural distance that the yoke metaphor can create today. By choosing “freeing” instead of the traditional “easy” (from the Greek chrēstos, meaning “kind” or “well-fitting”), this rendering emphasizes the release from guilt, shame, and religious oppression, inviting the weary soul into a life marked by divine companionship, transformative purpose, and shared strength. This approach aims to preserve the full depth of the original teaching while making it more immediately accessible to modern readers.
